next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

The roomette looked smaller after dark than it had on the platform in Reno, like the walls had inched inward once the train left the station lights. Eli sat sideways on the narrow seat with his bad rib turned toward the window and the green folder open across his knees. The fold-down table trembled with every joint in the track. The plastic cup of water on it kept rippling as if someone were tapping a finger against the rim.

He had labeled three envelopes in block letters before boarding: `CASH`, `RECEIPTS`, `DO NOT SAY THIS`. The last one already had five torn pages in it. He had started three different versions of the same sentence in his notebook and crossed all of them out hard enough to scar the paper. The train swayed left. His pen ran off the line. He wrote one more opening anyway.

Nora, I know this sounds rehearsed.

He tore that page out too.

If he started with how it sounded, he was already acting. If he started with why he left, he was already bargaining. He pressed his thumb into the old crack in the folder spine and looked at the receipts instead: two MoneyGram stubs from the grocery counter on South Virginia, one payroll cash-out slip from the casino office, and one handwritten list of dates Theo had made him keep. Each slip carried a date, amount, method, and confirmation number. Nothing lyrical fit there, and no room for mood remained.

A knock came against the sliding door. The car attendant, Randi, leaned in with the step stool tucked under one arm. She wore a denim vest over the railroad shirt and a brass pin shaped like a trout on the co...
open_hooks_to_carry: Restroom overdose response with procedural, bodily specificity
Post-crisis crash where Eli recognizes limits of private control
prev_tail_excerpt:
...self start to explain, heard the old compulsive logistics rising, and forced a stop.

"I miss you. That's all. Good night."

He ended the message and sat with the dark screen reflected in the motel window. Across the parking lot, a man in pajama pants smoked under the ice machine and watched the empty road like he expected someone to pull in and save him from his own choices.
He did not hit replay. He could not stand hearing himself ask for connection in that careful, work-safe tone.

Eli set the phone facedown, then picked it up again two minutes later to check for a response he knew would not be there. Nothing. He set an alarm for 8:30 p.m. and lay back without undressing, one hand over the taped rib as if he could hold the bone together by pressure alone.

The room heater kicked on and rattled like loose dice in a cup. He closed his eyes and ran the week's numbers in his head until they blurred: six hundred three net, five hundred sent, one hundred three left for rent, food, meds, tape, bus fare, and whatever would break next. The figures did not comfort him. They did not need to. They were factual, and facts had finally started moving in the same direction as guilt.

At 1:16 a.m., before he slept, he checked the phone again.

No new voicemail. No text. No missed call.

At 8:30 p.m. the alarm dragged him upright into the same ache, the same lint smell still trapped in his hair, the same badge waiting on the table by the door. Deena had texted the shift line at noon: NEED YOU FOR EXTRA AUDIT COVERAGE AFTER LAUNDRY. CONFIRM.

He typed back with his thumb.

`I'll be there.`
state_deltas_required: Keep earning and stay emotionally detached until he can return with money.
Eli calls Theo for help managing structured repayments and accepts strict relational terms instead of self-directed redemption.
